text: core objects S3 classes backed C++ external pointers (via Rcpp):
code: PJRTPlugin       – loaded shared library (.so/.dylib) providing the PJRT C API   └─ PJRTClient  – owns devices, creates buffers, compiles programs        └─ PJRTDevice  – a single device (e.g. "cpu:0", "cuda:1") PJRTProgram            – MLIR or HLO source code ready for compilation   └─ PJRTLoadedExecutable  – compiled, device-specific executable PJRTBuffer         – device-resident tensor (the main data object) PJRTArrayPromise   – async device-to-host transfer result (environment-based) PJRTElementType    – dtype enum (pred, i8–i64, ui8–ui64, f32, f64)

text: Plugins clients singletons cached global environment (plugin.R): pjrt_client(platform) checks cache, otherwise loads plugin creates client. title: Adding Custom Calls
text: {pjrt} ships built-custom calls registered automatically package loaded. includes print_tensor linear-algebra primitives geqrf / orgqr (QR factorisation), lu, svd, eigh. example uses print_tensor, takes single tensor operand, prints , returns nothing. stablehlo.custom_call op carries: call_target_name – string names handler registered PJRT runtime (, built-"print_tensor"). Note name also appears @print_tensor op name textual form; @<name> purely pretty-printer convention mirrors call_target_name, attribute string operative runtime. api_version = 4 : i32 – calling convention used invoke handler. Just set 4. has_side_effect = true – required ops exist side effect (printing). wouldn’t set TRUE , compiler might drop operation one optimization passes. backend_config = {...} (optional) – dictionary attributes handler can read. print_tensor accepts print_header (default "PJRTBuffer") print_footer (default "[ <dtype>{<shape>} ]"); pass "" either suppress line. operand_layouts / result_layouts indicate layout custom call expects outputs (e.g., row-major vs. col-major). XLA’s documented default newly-created shapes row-major (XLA Shapes, ’s best always set clarity. Next, discuss register custom call.
code: library(pjrt)  src <- r"( func.func @main(%x: tensor<2x3xf32>) -> tensor<2x3xf32> {   stablehlo.custom_call @print_tensor(%x) {     call_target_name = "print_tensor",     backend_config = {       print_header = "my matrix",       print_footer = "----"     },     has_side_effect = true,     api_version = 4 : i32,     operand_layouts = [dense<[1, 0]> : tensor<2xindex>],     result_layouts = []   } : (tensor<2x3xf32>) -> ()    "func.return"(%x) : (tensor<2x3xf32>) -> () } )"  exec <- pjrt_compile(pjrt_program(src)) buf  <- pjrt_buffer(matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3), dtype = "f32") out  <- pjrt_execute(exec, buf) ## my matrix ##  1 3 5 ##  2 4 6 ## ---- as_array(out) ##      [,1] [,2] [,3] ## [1,]    1    3    5 ## [2,]    2    4    6

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: {pjrt}’s build links LAPACK BLAS, CPU handler can call LAPACK / BLAS routine directly. LAPACK factorisation routines need working buffer whose optimal size depends input dimensions implementation-specific blocking parameters. standard idiom call routine twice: first workspace-size argument set -1, makes LAPACK skip actual work write optimal size first element workspace buffer; second time buffer size real factorisation. LAPACK signals success failure integer info -parameter (info = 0 success, anything else error). must checked every call – workspace query real call. {pjrt} provides small helper, lapack_check_info(info, routine_name) src/ffi_lapack.h, returns Error::Success() info == 0 Error::Internal("<routine_name> failed info = <info>") otherwise. Wrap LAPACK call PJRT_RETURN_IF_ERROR(lapack_check_info(info, "...")) XLA stops execution rather reading garbage results downstream. Windows pitfall: f32 LAPACK. R Windows uses bundled LAPACK, ships double-precision routines – single -precision available. handler calls single-precision LAPACK fail load Windows. macOS Linux unaffected (use system LAPACK, precisions). workaround promote f32 f64 around LAPACK call Windows; See existing linear algebra custom calls via C++ templates.

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: getting CUDA-specific handler details, note CUDA code pjrt structured differently “natural” CUDA backend. library based custom calls jaxlib, ships separate wheels CPU CUDA: jaxlib base CPU wheel, jax-cuda12-pjrt / jax-cuda12-plugin CUDA-specific wheels built --config=cuda CUDA toolkit build path. CUDA wheels can include CUDA SDK (Software Development Kit) headers, link CUDA libraries, use real types directly. R packages don’t equivalent mechanism. R CMD INSTALL builds one source tree one Makevars; “platform variant” way Python wheels CUDA extras. pjrt.binary load run machines without CUDA toolkit installed (even without NVIDIA GPU) – otherwise ’d break every CPU-user. pjrt’s CUDA path: never #includes CUDA SDK header, types every CUDA value opaque (void * streams handles, uintptr_t-aliased CUdeviceptr device addresses) don’t depend SDK’s typedefs compile time, maintains hand-rolled function-pointer table (CudaLibs ffi_cuda.h) cuSOLVER + CUDA driver entry points use, loads entry points lazily runtime via dlopen / dlsym inside get_cuda_libs() (ffi_cuda.cpp), package loads cleanly machines libcusolver.libcuda..1 aren’t present (loader just leaves table flagged unloaded, CUDA handlers return Error::Internal invoked). effect, CudaLibs + get_cuda_libs() job linker, dynamically hand – symbol lookup normal -lcusolver dependency system loader perform process startup, instead perform code runtime. trade-every cuSOLVER / driver function want use transcribed hand twice: typed function-pointer field CudaLibs (whose signature must match NVIDIA’s exactly – compiler can’t check us, since don’t include SDK headers tell expect), string symbol name passed dlsym loader. future, might move robust mechanism avoids redefinitions, e.g. including CUDA headers (might come licensing strings attached) e.g. generating declarations DSL.

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: CUDA concepts come every CUDA handler, briefly review . Host vs. device. CPU host GPU device. separate memory: host pointer can’t dereferenced device, device pointer can’t dereferenced host. Moving data requires explicit memcpy data can also directly copied device device. Streams. CUDA stream FIFO (first first ) queue operations device executes. Operations enqueued stream run order enqueued. Operations different streams can run concurrently dependency graph allows (e.g. memcpy one stream overlapping kernel another). Asynchronous execution. CUDA driver cuSOLVER calls – including *Async memcpys, kernel launches, cuSOLVER factorisations – non-blocking host. enqueue work stream return immediately, GPU actually done anything. host call’s return signals “now queued”; data isn’t real device gets point queue. source CUDA-handler pitfalls: read destination buffer right issuing async memcpy , ’ll see whatever , copy still queued. Stream synchronisation. cuStreamSynchronize(stream) blocks host every operation previously enqueued stream finished. ’s point “queued” turns “actually done” host’s perspective. Inside FFI handler, call cuStreamSynchronize unless genuinely need host-visible result – syncing forces host wait GPU breaks async pipelining XLA depends . place pjrt syncs today print_tensor’s CUDA path, copy data back host formatting . linalg handlers never sync: enqueue work stream return, XLA’s scheduling makes sure downstream op reads outputs runs kernel actually completed. Status codes. Every CUDA driver cuSOLVER call returns int status. 0 success; anything else error must propagated Error::Internal. dropped status doesn’t crash – leaves output buffer garbage surfaces much later wrong numerical answers, error message anywhere. PJRT_RETURN_IF_GPU_ERROR(expr, ) macro ffi_cuda.h wraps call site propagates non-zero statuses uniformly.

text: cuSOLVER loaded lazily via dlopen (see CUDA intro ), CUDA handler wants call cuSOLVER goes CudaLibs function-pointer table Solver prologue defined ffi_cuda.h. kernel declares Solver solver(get_cuda_libs()); solver.begin(scratch, stream); invokes routines. begin() borrows stream-bound handle pool (see stateful-handle paragraph ) allocates devInfo int device memory. cuSOLVER/CUDA handles expensive create, cached process-wide singleton pool – map stream pointer free-list handles, protected mutex concurrent host-side borrow / return. kernel borrows handle stream’s free-list (creating one first time), uses , returns scope exit; handles live process reused across pjrt_execute calls. design adapted jaxlib. cuSOLVER factorisation routines follow query / allocate / run idiom LAPACK (covered CPU section), workspace query separate function _bufferSize suffix (cusolverDnSgeqrf_bufferSize, etc.) rather lwork = -1 like LAPACK. Note computing buffer size cheap host-side computation. cuSOLVER actually two status mechanisms, two different failure modes: return value every routine host-side int returned synchronously call comes back, indicating whether call accepted queued onto stream. always check PJRT_RETURN_IF_GPU_ERROR. devInfo int device-side int cuSOLVER’s kernel writes execution, using convention LAPACK’s info (0 = success, <0 = illegal argument position |info|, >0 = routine-specific). split exists cuSOLVER async stream: host return covers pre-launch failures, actual runtime / numerical status can reported kernel run, kernel needs somewhere device-side write . pass devInfo , don’t read , forces synchronization. , follow jaxlib decision.

title: Adding Custom Calls
text: things aware implementing custom calls. Buffer aliasing. Stablehlo inherited XLA’s input-output aliasing. stablehlo.custom_call’s output_operand_aliases attribute lets XLA hand handler pointer input output. Routines overwrite input place read corrupted data. fix: copy input → destination buffer first, factor place , pointer-equality check copy skipped buffers already alias ((!= target) memcpy(...)). jaxlib uses pattern every linalg kernel; pjrt’s maybe_promote_inplace helper bakes guard CPU side. Dimension type mismatch byte-size overflow. Buffer dimensions arrive int64_t input.dimensions(), LAPACK cuSOLVER take int. naive cast wraps silently axes larger INT_MAX – use dim_to_int(dim, name, ) helper ffi_common.h, casts returns Error::InvalidArgument overflow. covers individual dimension, product: even dim_to_int widens m n int, expression like m * n * sizeof(T) still evaluates int arithmetic overflows large matrices (e.g. 50000 × 50000) result gets widened size_t. Cast least one operand size_t multiplication whole expression evaluates size_t: static_cast<size_t>(m) * n * sizeof(T). scratch.Allocate(0). ScratchAllocator::Allocate(0) returns std::nullopt, allocate_workspace<T> propagates Error::Internal. Downstream use custom calls prevent .

title: Get Started
text: {pjrt} package provides R interface PJRT, part OpenXLA project. PJRT portability layer allows frameworks work different hardware backends standardized interface. Anyone can implement PJRT plugin specific hardware backend, package currently supports CPU, NVIDIA GPU, Metal (Apple GPU; experimental) plugins. key design principle pjrt asynchronous dispatch: operations like buffer creation program execution return immediately PJRTBuffer objects may ready yet, allowing R continue preparing work device computes background. enables efficient overlap host device work, critical performance accelerators.

title: Get Started
text: creating plugin upon first use, shared library downloaded cached. plugin can used create client specific platform. Currently, exactly one client per platform stored global cache. client can one devices. relevant CPU clients, principle useful GPUs, although fully supported yet. important operations client supports data handling, compilation execution.
code: library(pjrt) plugin <- pjrt_plugin("cpu") plugin #> <PJRTPlugin:cpu> client <- plugin_client_create(plugin, "cpu") client #> <PJRTClient:cpu> the <- getFromNamespace("the", "pjrt") the[["clients"]][["cpu"]] #> <PJRTClient:cpu> the[["plugins"]][["cpu"]] #> <PJRTPlugin:cpu> cpu_devices <- devices(client) cpu_device <- cpu_devices[[1L]]

title: Get Started
text: Using client, can move data host (standard R objects) specific device create buffer. pjrt_buffer() returns PJRTBuffer immediately — buffer may ready yet (host--device transfer happens asynchronously), R block. Even transfer finishes, can inspect buffer metadata — operations non-blocking metadata available immediately: move data back host, use as_array(). blocks data available:
code: host_data <- 1:4 buf <- pjrt_buffer(host_data, device = cpu_device, shape = c(2L, 2L), dtype = "f32") buf #> PJRTBuffer  #>  1 3 #>  2 4 #> [ CPUf32{2x2} ] shape(buf) #> [1] 2 2 elt_type(buf) #> <f32> device(buf) #> <CpuDevice(id=0)> as_array(buf) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    1    3 #> [2,]    2    4

title: Get Started
text: PJRT compiles programs written HLO newer StableHLO device-specific executables. stablehlo package allows easily create StableHLO programs R. , define simple program adds two f32 tensors shape 2x2. First create program object, compile executable. Note compilation depends specific device.
code: src <- r"( func.func @main(   %x: tensor<2x2xf32>,   %y: tensor<2x2xf32> ) -> tensor<2x2xf32> {   %0 = "stablehlo.add"(%x, %y) : (tensor<2x2xf32>, tensor<2x2xf32>) -> tensor<2x2xf32>   "func.return"(%0): (tensor<2x2xf32>) -> () } )" program <- pjrt_program(src, format = "mlir") executable <- pjrt_compile(program, device = cpu_device) executable #> <PJRTLoadedExecutable>

title: Get Started
text: buffers executable ready, can run program. pjrt_execute() also returns PJRTBuffer immediately — R gets control back device computes background. Single outputs unpacked default; set simplify = FALSE always get list. retrieve result R array, call as_array():
code: x <- pjrt_buffer(c(1, 2, 3, 4), shape = c(2L, 2L), dtype = "f32") y <- pjrt_buffer(c(5, 6, 7, 8), shape = c(2L, 2L), dtype = "f32") result <- pjrt_execute(executable, x, y) result #> PJRTBuffer  #>   6 10 #>   8 12 #> [ CPUf32{2x2} ] as_array(result) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    6   10 #> [2,]    8   12

title: Get Started
text: running computations accelerators, synchronous approach creates synchronization bubbles — periods either host device sits idle waiting : async dispatch, host device can work parallel: pjrt, happens automatically. pjrt_buffer() pjrt_execute() return PJRTBuffer objects immediately without blocking R. buffer may ready yet, can pass directly operations. pay synchronization cost actually need host-side result (e.g. calling as_array()).
code: Host:   [prepare data] [wait...] [prepare data] [wait...] [prepare data] Device: [wait.........] [compute] [wait.........] [compute] [wait.........] Host:   [prepare batch 1] [prepare batch 2] [prepare batch 3] [prepare batch 4] Device:                   [compute 1......] [compute 2......] [compute 3......]

title: Get Started
text: PJRTBuffer transparent async type — can used directly operations even underlying computation hasn’t completed yet. PJRT handles dependencies internally. PJRTArrayPromise represents data transit device host. R arrays plain R objects (control like PJRTBuffer), need separate promise type represent “array isn’t ready yet.” two functions synchronization: await(x) — block PJRTBuffer ready device. Pure synchronization; data leaves device. Useful timing error checking, required passing buffer pjrt_execute(). value(x) — block PJRTArrayPromise completes, materialize return R array. get data device. types also support is_ready(x) non-blocking readiness checks.

title: Get Started
text: Buffers can passed directly operations without waiting — PJRT handles dependencies internally: can also chain execution async device--host transfer:
code: # Both transfers start immediately buf1 <- pjrt_buffer(matrix(1:4, 2, 2), dtype = "f32") buf2 <- pjrt_buffer(matrix(5:8, 2, 2), dtype = "f32")  # Execute with promise inputs — no need to wait for transfers result <- pjrt_execute(executable, buf1, buf2)  # Only block when we need the R array as_array(result) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    6   10 #> [2,]    8   12 src2 <- r"( func.func @main(%x: tensor<1000x1000xf32>) -> tensor<1000x1000xf32> {   %0 = "stablehlo.add"(%x, %x) : (tensor<1000x1000xf32>, tensor<1000x1000xf32>) -> tensor<1000x1000xf32>   "func.return"(%0): (tensor<1000x1000xf32>) -> () } )" exec2 <- pjrt_compile(pjrt_program(src2))  # Full async pipeline: transfer -> execute -> transfer back buf <- pjrt_buffer(matrix(runif(1e6), 1000, 1000), dtype = "f32") result <- pjrt_execute(exec2, buf) async_output <- as_array_async(result)   # returns immediately  # R can do other work here...  # Block only when we need the final R array output <- value(async_output) dim(output) #> [1] 1000 1000

title: Get Started
text: Blocking occurs : Calling await() buffer Calling as_array() buffer Calling value() PJRTArrayPromise Printing buffer (needs read values display ) Operations like shape(), elt_type(), device() block — buffer metadata available immediately.

title: Get Started
text: Bad — blocking every iteration: Better — log previous iteration’s metrics device computes next:
code: for (i in seq_len(n_iterations)) {   result <- pjrt_execute(executable, input)   metrics <- as_array(result)  # blocks! device idles during transfer   cat("Step", i, "loss:", metrics[1], "\n")   input <- prepare_next_batch()  # device still idle } prev_result <- NULL for (i in seq_len(n_iterations)) {   result <- pjrt_execute(executable, input)    if (!is.null(prev_result)) {     metrics <- as_array(prev_result)  # previous result likely ready     cat("Step", i - 1, "loss:", metrics[1], "\n")   }    input <- prepare_next_batch()  # overlap with device work   prev_result <- result }

title: Get Started
code: # Multiply-add program src3 <- r"( func.func @main(%x: tensor<100x100xf32>, %y: tensor<100x100xf32>) -> tensor<100x100xf32> {   %0 = "stablehlo.multiply"(%x, %y) : (tensor<100x100xf32>, tensor<100x100xf32>) -> tensor<100x100xf32>   %1 = "stablehlo.add"(%0, %x) : (tensor<100x100xf32>, tensor<100x100xf32>) -> tensor<100x100xf32>   "func.return"(%1): (tensor<100x100xf32>) -> () } )" exec3 <- pjrt_compile(pjrt_program(src3))  # Mini computation loop with chaining shp <- c(100L, 100L) x <- pjrt_buffer(matrix(runif(prod(shp)), shp[1], shp[2]), dtype = "f32") y <- pjrt_buffer(matrix(runif(prod(shp)), shp[1], shp[2]), dtype = "f32")  for (step in seq_len(5)) {   # Returns immediately — auto-waits for x and y if needed   result <- pjrt_execute(exec3, x, y)   # Chain: use result as input to next iteration   x <- result }  # Only retrieve the final value final <- as_array(result) cat("Final result shape:", dim(final), "\n") #> Final result shape: 100 100 cat("Final result[1,1]:", final[1, 1], "\n") #> Final result[1,1]: 22.14824

title: pjrt: R Interface to PJRT — pjrt-package
text: Configuration options provided XLA XLA provides various configuration options, documentation scattered across various websites. options include: TF_CPP_MIN_LOG_LEVEL: Logging level PJRT C++ API: 0: shows info, warnings errors 1: shows warnings errors 2: shows errors 3: shows nothing XLA_FLAGS: See openxla website information. Among , following flags control HLO dumping used inspect_hlo() inspect XLA IR: --xla_dump_to=<dir>: Directory XLA writes HLO dump files . --xla_dump_hlo_as_text: Dump HLO modules text (optimizations). XLA reads XLA_FLAGS , first compilation, set start fresh R session, e.g. Sys.setenv(XLA_FLAGS = "--xla_dump_to=/tmp/hlo --xla_dump_hlo_as_text"). Configuration options provided package PJRT_PLATFORM: Default platform use, falls back "cpu". PJRT_PLUGIN_PATH_<PLATFORM>: Path custom plugin library file specific platform (e.g., PJRT_PLUGIN_PATH_CPU, PJRT_PLUGIN_PATH_CUDA, PJRT_PLUGIN_PATH_METAL). set, package use path instead downloading plugin. PJRT_PLUGIN_URL_<PLATFORM>: URL download plugin specific platform (e.g., PJRT_PLUGIN_URL_CPU, PJRT_PLUGIN_URL_CUDA, PJRT_PLUGIN_URL_METAL). set, overrides default plugin download URL. PJRT_INSTALL: Controls whether plugins may downloaded automatically. Set "1" always download without asking (e.g. CI, scripts, Docker builds), "0" never download (call errors instructions instead). unset, package asks confirmation interactive session errors non-interactive one, script never triggers surprise download. PJRT_ZML_ARTIFACT_VERSION: Version ZML artifacts download. used downloading plugins zml/pjrt-artifacts. PJRT_CPU_DEVICE_COUNT: number CPU devices use. Defaults 1. primarily intended testing purposes. PJRT_CUDA_R_PACKAGE: Name R package providing CUDA libraries. Defaults value cuda12.8. Set use different CUDA toolkit package, note versions may work XLA plugin. PJRT_DEBUG: set (non-empty value), enables verbose debug output via cli::cli_inform().

code: The fix was to let the closure engine pin as well. `ClosureEngine` already delegates execution, output wrapping, and re-nesting wholesale to `r_fun`, so "place your own inputs on the device you compiled for" is one more clause in a contract that is already total delegation, not a new kind of trust. Under a pinned dispatcher the backend's `compile` callback is what chose the device, so the `r_fun` it returns closes over it: pjrt passes nothing extra and does nothing at execute time. Written into `?dispatcher`, including that `r_fun` sees only `$data` and not `$device`, so its placing must be idempotent.  Gone: the virtual predicate, the `move_inputs requires engine = "pjrt"` guard, and the `move_inputs` parameter on `Engine::run()`. The policy is now engine state, fixed at construction (`make_engine()`), because placing an input is the engine's own business -- only it knows what `$data` is. `Dispatcher::move_inputs_` stays: the core needs it for the key, the engine for the placing, and both are set from the one R-level argument.  **Deliberately not done: the DevicePolicy object (part 2 of the original plan).** Once the closure engine may pin, the coupling that motivated it is gone -- there is no longer an invariant for pjrt to enforce across "device leaves the key" and "inputs get placed", because a closure engine legitimately places nothing itself. What remains is one immutable bool used by two collaborators for two different jobs. Wrapping that in a polymorphic policy would have to reach into `PjrtEntry`'s client/device to do the placing (tighter coupling, not looser) and would add a virtual call per array leaf to a hot path with an explicit launch-overhead benchmark. Revisit only if a third device policy appears. The wrap material used to come from two sources: `out_tree` / `ambiguous_out` from the callback, but dtype and shape scavenged off the real output buffers on the entry's *first execution*. Now the backend single-sources the description of its own outputs -- the arrangement the closure engine already has by construction (`r_fun` wraps its own outputs; pjrt has no opinion). Chosen over reading PJRT's `PJRT_Executable_Output*` C API, which would be authoritative but is a pjrt-engine-only mechanism with no analogue for closure.  Payoff, all realized: `build_entry()` builds the templates on the cold path, the `if (pe->templates == R_NilValue)` branch is gone from the hot path, and `PjrtEntry` is immutable once constructed -- so `Engine::run()` now takes `const CacheEntry&`, which confines the `preserve()` rooting discipline to `build_entry`.  Trade-off accepted knowingly: the templates could not previously lie (they were read from the buffers the executable really produced). A backend whose declaration disagrees with its executable now stamps a wrong `$dtype`/`$shape` onto correct buffers. The one half pjrt can still settle is kept: `run()` checks the declared output count against the executable's actual one.  anvl (perf/dispatch-overhead) updated in lockstep: `compile_xla()` returns `out_avals`, and the AOT `xla()` path -- which wraps its own outputs and only wants the flags -- derives `ambiguous_out` from it. The core now builds the input sequence while it classifies (`ExecInput`: the SEXP, its aval, and whether it must be uploaded) and hands the engine exactly that. The count *is* `inputs.size()`; there is nothing to skip; and `Engine::run()` no longer takes a `CacheKey` at all -- the engine layer no longer knows what a cache key or a static argument is.  Protocol consequence: `r_fun` now receives only the dynamic leaves. It used to get the statics too and drop them itself, re-asserting they matched -- a check that could never fire, since the dispatcher keys statics with `identical()`, so a cache hit already proves it. anvl's quickr flat wrapper loses that block; the non-flat wrapper (no dispatcher in front of it) keeps `quickr_assert_static_args()`.
